chapters.title: Equal in name, not in daily life
chapters.text:
Teleportation is not real, but for a moment, let us picture moving a woman from two hundred years ago into the present day. She would be thrilled, because women can now go to work and earn a living. They can hold a credit card, attend college, and vote. Do not let these wins fool you, though. They are stunning, but sometimes they are only illusions, or at best the very first steps on a long, bumpy road to a society that is truly equal and free of toxic masculinity and old patriarchal norms.

In honesty, men still thrive. To see it for yourself, open Forbes magazine. In 2021, for example, the list of the wealthiest people on Earth included only white men. In 2022, only 6.2 percent of CEOs at Fortune 500 companies were women. Even without looking that closely, you can spot the pattern at the national level. The US has only had male presidents.

Gender equality is just a phrase, and women have to keep catching up.

Modern women can step onto the field, but the rules do not favor them. Beyond being smart and confident, the world demands that women perform emotional labor. They must be compassionate, empathetic, and kind, all of them so-called feminine traits. Are these traits bad or demeaning? No. Do they push women to put other people's feelings first and ignore their own needs and ambitions? Yes, absolutely, and in this summary, we will dig into this hidden burden that so many women carry.

chapters.title: Empathy is not owned by one gender
chapters.text:
Jennifer was looking forward to Mother's Day, the day she could finally sit back and have a little time to herself. Her hopes for rest fell apart, though, when all her relatives decided to gather for the anniversary of her father's death. Of course, all the preparation landed on Jennifer's shoulders, a person who was still grieving, whose schedule was already packed, and whose family could not afford to host a gathering. Still, Jennifer pushed her own feelings and comfort aside and started preparing. After all, she was a woman, and people expected that of her, to always work for the common good and create wonderful experiences for others.

Time is a precious asset, and giving most of it away is an unfortunate reality for many women.

This kind of emotional labor plays out in many families, and it goes unnoticed, because society links it to supposedly inherent feminine traits. People assume that only women can feel emotions and empathy. They shut down any complaints from women with lines like, but you are good at this, or with claims that men's brains are different from women's. None of these arguments hold up, and here is why.

The brain is not fixed by gender. Your brain shapes your behavior regardless of biological sex. It reads all the social cues and cultural context around you and adjusts. Women's brains are shaped by the results of patriarchy, so many women see themselves as lesser and as naturally suited to emotional labor.

Men are capable of empathy and emotional labor. Many are afraid to admit it, but they can do it when they have the right motivation. In one experiment, men were offered money every time they gave an empathetic answer that scored well. The financial reward pushed them to match the high scores of women.

Now, think about double standards. Even a small amount of emotional labor from a man earns praise. People are amazed at a man who gives up some of his precious time to be with his family. They celebrate him for playing with and caring for his own child. Women, on the other hand, spend 37 percent more time than men on emotional labor. And in a bitter twist, their efforts are not only unpaid, they are also underappreciated and unseen by others.

chapters.title: Lovely on the outside, unheard underneath
chapters.text:
What pops into your head when you see your male coworker rushing through the halls with a serious look? Most likely, you assume he is very busy and deep in his work. What about when you see a woman with the same look? You may not think about it, but most people will read that woman as hostile. Why is she not smiling?

During the 2016 presidential campaign, people criticized Hillary Clinton for exactly that reason. They shamed her for not being smiley and approachable enough. Hillary also said in her documentary that she had to pay close attention to her makeup and hairstyle just to be seen as presentable. She calculated that those visual preparations took around 25 days of her 600-day campaign. Her male opponent, obviously, did not have to do that.

Society tells men to rein in their emotions, yet they enjoy the greatest freedom to express themselves.

Stepping into the spotlight is a win for women, especially when they want to make their case. But more often than not, when they are exposed to media and public attention, they become the target of scrutiny and sexism. Sexism usually comes in two forms.

Hostile sexism is the loudest kind. It includes all the stereotypes and prejudices society holds about women and feels the need to express in an aggressive way. Hostile sexist men say women are unfaithful and cunning. They do not see women as leaders or managers. They are afraid of their supposed manipulation schemes. Hostile sexism is everywhere, and it is even fashionable among some men.

Benevolent sexism is much quieter. Benevolent sexist men compliment women and call them baby or girl, and they praise them for being pure and modest. But one slip from the desired feminine behavior, like revealing clothes or unacceptable sexual preferences, and these men are quick to punish and humiliate women. This form of sexism is the most damaging, because it sets very high standards for women and fuels hatred of women across society.

The whole setup lets us blame and dehumanize women for the very thing we are so eager to watch, as Rose Hackman has written.

chapters.title: What caring really costs
chapters.text:
Many women feel like they have dozens of tabs open in their minds, most of them about taking care of children. Children do need a lot of emotional labor, which is understandable. They are little humans who cannot carry their own emotional load and need a caring hand. Kids also need cuddles, kisses, jokes, answers to their questions, and good food. In other words, they need a lot of attention. Some women take on this challenge themselves, while others hire nannies to share the emotional labor. Both choices are perfectly fine, and it comes down to personal preference.

Women's worries are real, and brushing them off is absurd.

The culture of nannying goes back to a terrible page in American history, slavery. Back then, some wealthy white women would look for enslaved Black women who had just given birth and force them to leave their own families to care for the white infants. It turned into a vicious cycle. Black women were giving birth to more enslaved people while also caring for the next generation of enslavers.

Because of that history, a powerful trope in American culture took hold. It was the figure of mammy, a large Black woman who was always smiling and comforting. In movies and books, these women were always near their white families, and they seemed to have no personal life or connection to the Black community. In real life, that was rarely true, but the trope served its purpose. It was used to justify the myth that Black women were naturally suited to domestic service.

Today, women of color make up a large share of the nanny workforce, and they still hold very little power over their work. In fact, 23 percent of domestic workers are underpaid, which is illegal. Nannies can do little about it, because they still do not have enough labor protections. So whatever comes their way, whether it is a toxic working environment, sexual harassment, or low wages, they accept it to make a living.

The belief that love and empathy in action are not work is what holds back labor rights and fair pay, as Rose Hackman has written.

chapters.title: Boys will be human, full stop
chapters.text:
Boys will be boys. That phrase is like a magic spell that instantly softens men's bad behavior. Got drunk and trashed the apartment? Boys will be boys. Bullied their female classmates or coworkers? Boys will be boys. In 2013, two male teenagers who were star football players at their school were found guilty of sexually harassing a girl in Ohio. Someone shared the footage of their crime online, and the case blew up. No matter how grim the crime was, though, the parents and coaches sided with the boys. After all, they were the pride of the school. And once again, boys will be boys.

You cannot build a bridge to equality when only one side is supported.

Some men are annoyed by feminist ideas. When they try to join the conversation, they often ask, but what about men? Men go to war, they take on more high-risk activities and jobs, and they have higher rates of depression and suicide. Those facts are true, but this is not a competition over who has it worse. It is proof that the patriarchy and the hierarchical order fail men too.

For men, emotional labor is taboo. Instead, they believe they should develop the qualities of a man box. In one study, 1000 men from the US, the United Kingdom, and Mexico named these qualities as self-sufficiency, toughness, physical attractiveness, straightness, homophobia, hypersexuality, avoidance of feminine tasks, dominance over women, and the belief that aggression toward women can be justified. If men do not follow these rules, their masculinity is questioned.

Studies showed that 40 percent of men who identified with the man box and decided to switch off their emotional, so-called weaker centers had thoughts about suicide. In contrast, only 17 percent of men who did not relate to these principles considered it. These numbers change everything and prove one important point. Men and women need emotional connection and empathy equally. Men who are emotionally tied to their families, friends, and community have much better physical and mental health, and they have a positive effect on the people around them.

Men who lost their wives unexpectedly are at 70 percent higher risk of dying prematurely than those whose wives passed away with some warning.

chapters.title: Time to talk it through
chapters.text:
Will emotional labor ever get the public attention it deserves? Will women around the world finally get the recognition they have earned? It is hard to say, given how much of a blow it can land on the patriarchal order of our society. Still, hope remains, and the answer comes through communication.

If you are in a long-term romantic relationship or marriage, here is the key thing to remember. It is never too late to change how your relationship works. Here is how you can do it.

Talk with your partner about the family responsibilities you both share. Who does more emotional labor? Who gives up more? Who gets more benefits from the relationship? Then, think about possible solutions and how you can build more balance into your daily life. Decide who will plan social events, organize family meetings, and take care of the kids.

Let go of the idea of compulsive caretaking. Society suggests that only women have to perform certain tasks. They sometimes do this without thinking, even when their partner offers to help. If that happens to you, try to notice this habit. You can finally set boundaries and practice self-care only when you know when you are putting your own needs aside.

Life gets easier when you accept that you cannot please everyone.

Married women are especially at risk of burning out from emotional labor. They are 40 percent more likely to report a high level of stress than men. Married women also feel the urge to cry more often because of stress, in 54 percent of married women, compared to 33 percent of single women. And finally, all of these women say that life would be much easier if someone offered them a helping hand with all their chores. So, this is your sign to have an honest conversation with your partner.

When it comes to the workplace or communities, these steps can push emotional labor forward. Recognize that emotional labor is just as important as other soft skills. Distribute tasks that include emotional labor fairly across all genders. Build open communication and feedback so women can share what is bothering them.

chapters.title: Wrapping up
chapters.text:
When COVID hit the world in 2020, many women left their jobs to stay home and care for their families. Oxfam calculated that this gesture was worth 10.8 trillion dollars a year to the US, more than the tech industry. Impressive numbers, but how little recognition they get. That is why we need to raise awareness about emotional labor. Money is valuable, but so is time and the sense of connection and empathy that many women bring to the people around them.

Try this

Ask a woman you know if she needs help. Even a small act of kindness can lift her day. Share your knowledge with others. Post about emotional labor on your social media. Talk about it with your family and friends, and bring it up in your romantic relationship. Break the stereotype. Try giving men and women the same amount of emotional labor in your household or workplace.

Did you read all the way to the end?
